title: David Wessman
content:
{{Top|rwp}} {{Person |image=[[File:Davidwessman.jpg]] |name=David Wessman |birth= |death= |listedpronouns= |nationality= |occupation=[[Wikipedia:Video game developer|Game developer]] |companies= |website= }} {{Quote|The limitations with the technology made it difficult to be realistic because, for instance, if the Star Destroyer shows up to launch TIE fighters at you, it's not going to "piecemeal" them out three at a time. It'll swamp you with a few squadrons. From the individual starfighter pilot's point of view, though, I think they came out pretty well.|David Wessman|}} '''David "Wotan" Wessman''' is a [[Wikipedia:Video game developer|video game developer]], who is best known for his work on the [[X-Wing series (computer games)|X-Wing computer game series]]. His industry career began in 1991 as a [[Wikipedia:Game tester|tester]] at [[LucasArts]] where he met [[Lawrence Holland]] and expressed his interest in joining Holland's team. He was subsequently hired as the internal test lead for the [[Star Wars video games|video game]] ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing]]'' and was a co-founder of what later became [[Totally Games]]. David soon transitioned to development, becoming a mission builder and lead writer; roles he would continue with on [[Star Wars: TIE Fighter (video game)|''Star Wars: TIE Fighter'']]. During this time he also co-authored the official strategy guides for both games, working with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and fellow mission builder [[David Maxwell]]. He was promoted to lead mission designer on ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing vs. TIE Fighter]]'' and "gameplay & story lead" on ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing Alliance]]''. After leaving Totally Games, Wessman was a lead or senior designer on ''Blood Wake'' (2001), ''Chronicles of Riddick: Escape from Butcher Bay'' (2004), ''Saints Row'' (2006) and ''Death Jr. 2: Root of Evil'' (2006). In 2008 he left the game industry to teach game design at the University of Advancing Technology in Tempe, AZ. As a [[Wikipedia:Professor|professor]] in game design he has taught at the graduate and undergraduate level, and since 2015 is a lecturer at [https://www.buas.nl/games Breda University of Applied Sciences] in The Netherlands, but he also continues to develop games and his most recent effort is a hard science fiction space combat simulator called ''[https://intheblack.gg In The Black]''. ==Biography== An avid player of all types of games, but especially [[Star Wars video games|video game]]s, David "Wotan" Wessman''[[TIE Fighter: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' began working in the [[Wikipedia:Video game industry|game industry]] in [[1991]], taking a job as a [[Wikipedia:Game tester|tester]] at [[LucasArts]], then known as [[Lucasfilm Ltd.|Lucasfilm games]].{{WebCite|url=http://www.dgxpo.com/speaker.htm|text=Speakers|work=DGXPO.com|archivedate=20080612181338}} During his time at LucasArts, Wessman tested the [[Wikipedia:Adventure game|adventure games]] [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis|''Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis'']] and [[Wikipedia:Monkey Island 2: LeChuck's Revenge|''Monkey Island 2: LeChuck's Revenge'']],{{LinkedIn|url=in/wessmaniac|text=David Wessman|archiveurl=https://archive.vn/6BNd}} along with [[Lawrence Holland]]'s [[Wikipedia:World War II|World War II]] [[Wikipedia:Flight simulator|flight simulator]] [[Wikipedia:Secret Weapons of the Luftwaffe|''Secret Weapons of the Luftwaffe'']], and its [[Wikipedia:Expansion pack|expansion packs]]. In [[1992]], Wessman joined Holland's independent development team, (which would eventually become [[Totally Games]]) as the internal test lead for ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing]]'', a ''[[Star Wars]]'' flight simulator the team were developing for LucasArts,. He was joined by fellow tester and longtime friend [[David Maxwell]]. Together they both took on additional writing and mission building responsibilities and transitioned from being testers to being developers. The overall story of the game was developed by lead developers [[Lawrence Holland]] and [[Edward Kilham]]. Wessman and Maxwell were otherwise able to design their own scenarios, creating more than twice as many missions as were required and selecting the best ones from those available to include in the final game.''[[X-Wing: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' [[File:MaxwellWessman.jpg|thumb|left|200px|[[David Maxwell]] and David Wessman]] One of Wessman's primary concerns was ensuring that all missions could be beaten—when player's failed, he provided a debriefing to offer tips on where the player went wrong. Following the release of ''X-Wing'' in [[February]] [[1993]], Wessman worked with Maxwell and [[Rusel DeMaria]] to co-author ''[[X-Wing: The Official Strategy Guide]]''. The book featured the story of the player character, [[Keyan Farlander/Legends|Keyan Farlander]], as written by DeMaria. Wessman and Maxwell contributed mission strategies and general tactics, along with most of the [[Wikipedia:Screenshot|screenshots]] in the book. When the team began working on ''X-Wing''{{'s}} [[1994]] sequel, [[Star Wars: TIE Fighter (video game)|''Star Wars: TIE Fighter'']], Wessman moved into working full time on mission design. He continued to work with Maxwell to design missions for the game and the pair again contributed strategies to the subsequent ''[[TIE Fighter: The Official Strategy Guide]]''. Wessman remained with Totally Games until [[2000]], acting as lead mission designer for [[1997]]'s ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing vs. TIE Fighter]]'' and the gameplay and story lead on [[1999]]'s ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing Alliance]]''. ==Works== ===Bibliography=== {|{{Prettytable}} ! Year||Title||Format||Publisher |- |[[1993]]||''[[X-Wing: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' (with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and [[David Maxwell]])||Strategy guide||[[Prima Games]] |- |[[1994]]||''[[TIE Fighter: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' (with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and [[David Maxwell]])||Strategy guide||[[Prima Games]] |- |[[1994]]||''[[TIE Fighter: Defender of the Empire: Official Secrets & Solutions]]'' (with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and [[David Maxwell]])||Strategy guide||[[Prima Games]] |- |[[1995]]||''[[X-Wing Collector's CD-ROM: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' (with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and [[David Maxwell]])||Strategy guide||[[Prima Games]] |- |[[1995]]||''[[TIE Fighter Collector's CD-ROM: The Official Strategy Guide]]'' (with [[Rusel DeMaria]] and [[David Maxwell]])||Strategy guide||[[Prima Games]] |} ===Gameography=== {|{{Prettytable}} ! Year||Title||Contribution||Publisher||Platform |- |[[1993]]||''[[Star Wars: X-Wing (video game)|Star Wars: X-Wing]]''||Mission Design (with [[David Maxwell]])
Lead Tester||[[LucasArts]]||[[Wikipedia:Personal computer|PC]] |- |[[1994]]||''[[Star Wars: TIE Fighter (video game)|Star Wars: TIE Fighter]]''||Mission Design||LucasArts||PC |- |[[1997]]||''[[Star Wars: X-Wing vs. TIE Fighter]]''||Mission Design Lead||LucasArts||PC |- |[[1999]]||''[[Star Wars: X-Wing Alliance]]''||Mission Lead & Story Design||LucasArts||PC |} ==Notes and references== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{LinkedIn|url=in/wessmaniac|text=David Wessman|archiveurl=https://archive.vn/6BNd}} *{{IMDb|name/nm2304854}} *{{MobyGames|url=person/1674/david-wessman|text=David Wessman}} [[Category:Non-fiction authors]] [[Category:Other Lucasfilm employees]] [[Category:Star Wars game developers]]
